data.title: Alpha Hunters - How To Navigate The Potential Recession And Position Your Portfolio
data.description:
Introduction

As a U.S.-based investor, every day it seems like there's more uncertainty surrounding the economy. As a result, the FED recently left rates unchanged to no one's surprise.

I was personally looking for a potential cut sometime in the back half of the year with the new chair coming on in May. But now, a rate cut seems to move farther and farther away as the war with Iran rages on.

Moreover, the market seems to now be pricing in fears of a possible recession. While the war doesn't guarantee we'll see one, I do believe the longer it goes on, the likelihood of one increases.

And with the recent attack from Iran sending oil & gas prices higher, the market continues to sell off, with the S&P (SP500) down close to 2% at the time of writing. Other commodities like gold (GLD) and Bitcoin (BTC-USD) are also down, while crude oil is up close to 4%.

While continued market volatility is likely with investors turning to cash, some of us are staying put. I'm a believer in dollar-cost averaging into positions that I have high conviction in instead of trying to time the market.

In this article, I discuss one particular sector I like, one to avoid, and what other places to look to help investors navigate a potential recession.

Why I Believe We Will See A Recession Soon

I know some will read this and say, Oh my god, we've been hearing about a potential recession for years now.

While we have, I think the chances are much more likely now. Earlier this month, CNBC reported that recession odds jumped from around 22% to 35% due to oil going above $100 a barrel.

And after closing up more than 16% in 2025, the S&P's performance so far this year has been disappointing. Currently, the index is down over 3%, and I think underperformance is likely to continue for at least the near term.

This seems mainly due to the war with Iran. Iran has mentioned that it has no plans of slowing down or declaring peace.

While no one knows if Israel, Iran, and the U.S. can come to an agreement soon, I don't believe it will happen in the near future by the looks of things. And this is despite President Trump's announcement that a deal may be reached soon.

In the coming months, if the war is still ongoing, I believe recession odds will increase further. By this summer I wouldn't be surprised if the odds are above 50%. And as odds increase, it's likely the market will continue to sell off.

How much? I can't say. But I've been preparing my portfolio by having cash ready to deploy. I've also been repositioning by selling shares in companies in which I have made significant profits.

For perspective, I sold some of my shares in one of my longest-held stocks, Agree Realty (ADC). I started buying ADC heavily in October 2023 when the stock was trading in the low $50s. Due to my cost basis of around $58, I took profit around $80 a share.

Another stock I took profit in is Altria (MO), another one of my longest holdings. I was buying this stock in the low $40s and have a cost basis around $43.

Honestly, I've been thinking of selling out of my entire position in Altria. Not because of the fundamentals, but because of my gains and the potential to buy it back at a much lower price.

But this may be a decision for another article. Without further ado, let's get into how investors should navigate a possible recession.

Invest for Income

If you follow me, then you probably know what I'm going to say. Times like now are great to become a dividend-focused investor.

However, dividend stocks are not immune to economic downturns and will experience sell-offs as well. The chart below shows how dividend stocks performed in previous recessions.

You can see how they outperformed from 1981 to 1982 saw less of a drawdown in 2001 and during the Great Financial Crisis than the overall market.

The chart below shows the difference in how dividend growth stocks performed vs those that offered a mix vs strictly income stocks.

Dividend growth stocks performed the best overall with higher returns in the 1990-1991 recession and saw less of a drawdown during the GFC and COVID.

While each recession is unique, I believe dividend stocks can again outperform the broader markets. But this depends on how you position your portfolio. Just going out and buying any stock that pays a dividend does not guarantee portfolio outperformance.

So, instead of just investing for income, be specific about your positions. I like to first look to the REIT (XLRE) sector. No, I'm not just saying this because of my affiliation with iREIT+Hoya Capital.

I'm saying this because many REITs are recession-resistant depending on their subsectors and tenant concentration. For instance, although I trimmed my ADC position, I believe they're one of the best-positioned REITs for a recession.

With 67% exposure to investment-rated tenants and those who offer essential services to consumers like Walmart (WMT), AutoZone (AZO), and Home Depot (HD), it's likely the REIT will continue to collect on its rent.

Big brother Realty Income (O) is another REIT that's likely to do well despite less exposure to investment-rated tenants. This is due to their diverse tenant base that spans across more than 15,000 tenants.

I believe their size & scale advantages are enough to keep the dividend safe. For context, O has never cut its dividend, even as a private company. In total, they have more than 50 years of consecutive dividend increases.

And I believe these will continue even in a recession. Additionally, both ADC and Realty Income pay dividends monthly, something that can help pay the bills or at least reduce the impact in a downturn.

Another REIT I like is CareTrust REIT (CTRE). Healthcare stocks typically perform well in times of uncertainty, as the need for healthcare continues no matter the economic environment. CTRE remains in a great position with their low-leveraged balance sheet of just 0.7x.

This gives them plenty of room for acquisitions and to sustain the dividend should times become tough. While they don't pay monthly like ADC & O, I do believe they could experience less of a drawdown because of their subsector.

There are plenty of other sectors that are recession-resistant but don't offer as high of a dividend yield as REITs.

What Sector I Would Avoid For Now

When looking for income, it's easy for someone to get caught up in chasing yield. If you're an income investor like myself, then it's likely you're guilty of this, the same as I have been in the past.

While the BDC (BIZD) sector currently offers some attractive buying opportunities as many have fallen from their highs due to lower interest rates, I would avoid the sector for now if you could.

While there are some BDCs that can reward you with income, I suspect many could reduce their dividends in a severe recession. This is because the likelihood of seeing borrowers default on loans is likely to increase.

BDCs typically lend to smaller, private, non-investment-rated tenants. And this increases their ability to default during recessions, in my honest opinion. You can see how private credit defaults have already skyrocketed, significantly higher than during the GFC.

This puts a strain on a BDC's dividend-paying ability and could result in a dividend cut. In the past year, many have been forced to reduce their dividends due to economic uncertainty. And in a recession, I think we would see even more.

One BDC that has never cut its dividend and went public during the GFC is Main Street Capital (MAIN). While the stock is much more attractive due to their pullback and pays a monthly dividend with a yield above 7%, I would still look to avoid BDCs altogether at this time.

If you're a current holder, I'm not saying sell your investments. But if you're looking to best position your portfolio for a recession, I would look elsewhere for income.

Other Places To Look For Income

If you're someone who's not a fan of REITs, then consider dividend growth stocks with long track records and recession-resistant businesses.

Two in particular I like are Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA). Recently both have been plagued by other headwinds like the president's threat to place a 10% cap on credit card companies.

But I still believe both have the ability to weather a recession. This is because their business model as a payment processor alleviates them from holding debt.

Although, volumes and transaction growth are likely to slow. The reason being is if we do see a recession, credit card transactions are likely to decline due to fewer swipes from consumers.

Furthermore, both are attractively priced right now. Visa hit a new 52-week low of $298.50 at the time of this article. Mastercard trades at $488 a share, not too far from their 52-week low of $465.59.

I've always preferred buying Visa below $300. Back in September, I wrote an article suggesting investors wait for a pullback below that price. I also wrote an article in December, suggesting that the company's price behavior was hinting at a possible recession.

While this was just an opinion, the sideways trading suggested the economy was cracking. Visa and Mastercard are two of the highest-quality growth stocks one can buy. And although they are likely to see volatility in a recession, their dividends will likely remain safe.

And going forward, I expect both to continue rewarding shareholders. Both usually have payout ratios in the teens, giving them plenty of room for future dividend increases. Throw in their A-rated balance sheets, and both could reward investors over the longer term.

Another way to position your portfolio is by holding dividend-paying ETFs. Once someone who scoffed at the idea of owning ETFs, as I preferred individual stocks, ETFs can help balance your portfolio and mitigate downside risks.

This is because of their structure, already diversified with multiple, high-quality companies. I personally like Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (CGDV).

Although they don't have as high of a yield as the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), another ETF I like, CGDV's multi-manager and active approach gives them a competitive advantage.

In the chart below, you can see CGDV has experienced less of a drawdown compared to the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO). SCHD has performed exceptionally thanks to their concentration in the energy (XLE) sector.

When it comes to positioning your portfolio, it's not all about beating the market's returns. It's also about mitigating downside and protecting your capital during market meltdowns. If your overall portfolio is in the red but sees less downside than the broader market, to me that's a win.

Risks and Conclusion

All stocks, sectors, and funds named in this article are still subject to downside if we see a recession.

But due to their fundamentals, higher yields, dividend pay dates, and portfolio concentrations, I suspect they all could see less of a drawdown vs the broader market, all while delivering reliable & stable income.

As a result, this helps protect an investor's capital and blunt the blow during huge market meltdowns. During a recession, I believe focusing on income is key to maintaining a successful portfolio.

If you're someone looking for how to position your portfolio for a potential recession, then consider these income picks.

data.title: Winnebago Industries: Those Who Take Risks After The Selloff May Win With New Gains
data.description:
It has only been two months since the publication of my previous coverage, and yet, Winnebago Industries, Inc. (WGO) had already dropped by 24% in value. I understand the cautious stance of the market due to the mounting cost pressures and emerging headwinds that may affect the RV or recreational vehicle market. Even so, this has opened new buying opportunities for us at this level as valuation and fundamentals remain attractive. Technicals present a bearish trend, but overselling proves that buying opportunities are there.

WGO Q2 2026: Smoothly Driving With Sustained Growth And Margins

Inflationary headwinds and new tariff woes continue to affect the demand across multiple industries. Even so, the RV market has shown recent improvements with higher shipments and strategic pricing. Key industry players like Winnebago Industries also takes advantage of recent market conditions with its strategic product diversification and vast market coverage. I'm sure we all saw this in its report yesterday.

In Q2 2026, its operating revenue amounted to $657.4M, up by 6.0% YoY from $620.2M. This YoY growth was weaker than in my previous coverage at 12.3%. But the positive trend means WGO still sustained its growth despite volatile macroeconomic conditions. If you look at its performance per segment, you can say that WGO remains prudent with its product mix. It made the right move to focus more on its Motorhome RV segment in line with the strong market demand for this RV type. As you can see, the segment revenue rose by 29.3% YoY, completely offsetting the weakness in the Towable RV segment. It delivered 1,1518 units versus 1,144 YoY. This was driven by its cheaper pricing strategies. If you divide net revenues by the units, the average price was $200,724 versus $205,944 YoY. But this $5,000 price reduction led to higher shipments.

Meanwhile, it enjoyed its stronger pricing power in the Marine segment. As you can see, its boat deliveries decreased by 54 units to 992 during the quarter. However, it had an average revenue of $79,838 per boat versus $78,108 YoY. This shows that it curates its products and sets their prices based not only on demand and cost pressures but also on consumer behavior or interest.

Moreover, its COGS and SG&A increased but remained manageable. Also, revenue growth remained enough to cover them. In the end, its operating margin still improved to 1.8% versus 1.2% YoY.

Potential Challenges

Despite its impressive results, I believe that WGO must still be careful with stubborn inflation as it remains above the 2.0% target. This may be exacerbated by new tariffs since they tend to raise domestic prices. WGO may also incur higher expenses for importing spare parts and materials needed for its RVs and boats. It may also have to raise wages to help employees cope with inflation. This may affect its pricing flexibility just like what we saw in its towable and motorhome pricing trends last Q4 2025.

Another consideration is the skyrocketing oil prices today. This will most likely raise fuel prices, which will also make RVing and boating more expensive. On top of that, high fuel prices may affect multiple industries, which can amplify inflation. So, it must watch out for its operating costs and margins more in H2 2026.

Mitigants

On the brighter side, I think WGO has some strengths and sprinkles of hope to defend itself from the headwinds.

One aspect to help it is the substitution effect. Yes, RVing may become more expensive this year. But this will be probably much cheaper than traveling by air or water and staying in hotels. Domestic travelers may opt for cheaper options like RV trips to spend time with family while balancing work with vacation. So, I think it will not totally lose its market appeal and demand. This may be supported by the lingering impact of the recent policy easing cycle, which may partially delay the impact of the potential headwinds explained earlier. Another thing to remember is that RVs target affluent and/or high-income individuals or households. This means that its target market is typically inflation-resistant. So, demand may remain resilient.

WGO may capitalize on its successful product mix and pricing strategies based on recent market trends. As you can see, Towable RV shipments in the market dropped by 13.2%, but Motorhome RV shipments rose by 12.7% YoY. So, I think WGO may take advantage of this situation by increasing its focus on Motorhomes rather than Towables. It can make more price adjustments to stabilize demand just like it did recently.

Lastly, its stellar Balance Sheet may help ensure its longevity. As you can see, its cash and cash equivalents dropped to $47.4M. But it was also able to reduce its debt by about $100M. In addition, the maturity profile is still decent since no amount will mature within a year. To be more specific, more or less 80% of the total has a maturity of five years. This is in line with the Net Debt/EBITDA of 3.0x, which means that WGO only needs 3.0 years theoretically to repay the whole amount even if it doesn't have to. This proves that it still has enough buffer to sustain its operating capacity and dividends.

Valuation

To reassess its valuation, I decided to update its Dividend Discount Model. Its annualized dividend is still $1.40, so the dividend growth rate also remains at 9.03%. Meanwhile, I still set its risk premium at 7.0% to be consistent with my previous coverage. This also makes my estimation conservative since this is higher than the standard of 5.0%. But note that the risk-free rate is now 4.3% in line with the 10-year treasury yield. Lastly, my estimated Bata is now 1.12, which means that the stock has become more volatile than in my previous coverage. This also means that WGO is more volatile than the S&P 500. With all this details, the TP or target price is $45.05. This is much lower than my previous TP. However, this is still much higher than the current price. With that, it is safe to say that WGO has already priced in the risks I have explained earlier.

I also used price ratios to value the stock. In P/E, WGO trades at 25.32x its EPS versus the five-year average of 31.89x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP will be $41.13. If you look at the price and EPS trend carefully, the sharp drop from 2023 to 2024-2026 was justifiable. This also shows the strong correlation between the stock price and the EPS. Nonetheless, the price drop to this level already appears a bit stretched. Also, the EPS has improved if you look at the increasing trend from 2024 to 2026. With that, it is logical for the price to increase again to the TP. At its current price, WGO is already properly valued. The same applies to P/S using sales as it suggests a TP of $47.48.

Technicals

WGO's technicals are still bearish due to the sharp downtrend, and the price still barely recovers despite the strong results. It is also trending below the 50-day and 200-day SMAs, showing that the current momentum is weaker than the average in the past 50-days. The 50-day SMA has already shifted downwards, suggesting that recent market sentiments have weakened. On a lighter note, there is still a possibility of a rebound. The most recent low of $32.57 did not break below the $31.83 support. There are also some buying volume, which means that buyers took advantage of the discounted price.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI show new buying opportunities. You can see that the MACD line is shifting upwards and has already crossed above the signal line, which can tell us that buying volume is increasing again. The histogram is also back to positive territory, which implies that buying volume has outpaced selling volume recently. Of course, I still advise some caution since it is still close to zero or neutral, so a rebound is not fully sure. Lastly, RSI is increasing again after the stock became oversold. This may open new entry opportunities and attract new buyers.

Conclusion

Winnebago Industries remains robust with its well-balanced growth and margins, driven by its prudent product mix and pricing strategy. It also maintains adequate liquidity to sustain its operating capacity and dividends. Its valuation is attractive again after the selloff priced in current market headwinds. Technicals adhere to it as the trend presents new buying opportunities after the recent overselling. I'm reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

data.title: BlackRock Advantage Large Cap Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Commentary as of 12/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of 5.43% (Institutional shares) and 5.35% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the fourth quarter of 2025.
  • The fund's outperformance of its benchmark was driven by sentiment-related insights, which captured favorable market positioning. Fundamental valuation and macro-related measures contributed to returns. In particular, sentiment indicators related to market positioning and investor flows proved effective. Fundamental valuation signals added to performance by identifying attractively priced names, while macroeconomic industry insights were also beneficial.
  • From a sector-positioning perspective, the fund remained largely neutral. However, there were slight overweight positions in the information technology (IT) and consumer discretionary sectors, and slight underweight holdings in the utilities and energy sectors.

Contributors

Sentiment was the strongest contributor. Notably, insights looking at short-selling activity from informed market participants were helpful through an overweight allocation to the consumer staples sector and an underweight exposure to the health care sector. These were supported by renewed retail investor enthusiasm and expectations of lower interest rates. Fundamental valuation signals focused on operating income drove successful stock selection in the industrials sector, which benefited from broader economic growth and fiscal policy shifts. Macro industry timing insights also added value.

Detractors

A few signals detracted modestly during the quarter. A macro signal that bets against short interest levels performed weakly in the IT sector by motivating an unsuccessful overweight exposure during a more defensive market rotation. Additionally, a quality-oriented signal predicting sales growth struggled in the communication services sector. Macro insights looking at historical patterns fell short amid rapid market shifts tied to fiscal policy and consumer behavior.

Portfolio management

Raffaele Savi, Travis Cooke,

Richard Mathieson

Top 10 holdings (%)

Alphabet (GOOG) 4.18
JPMorgan Chase (JPM) 2.99
Amazon.com (AMZN) 2.87
Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) 2.66
Walmart Inc (WMT) 2.42
Bank of America (BAC) 2.01
Charles Schwab Corporation (the) (SCHW) 1.48
Micron Technology Inc (MU) 1.44
Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) 1.42
Parker Hannifin Corporation (PH) 1.36

Investment approach

Invests at least 80% of its assets in equity securities or other financial instruments that are components of, or have market capitalizations similar to, the securities included in the Russell 1000 Value ® Index.

Further insight

Returns struggled in October amid rising tail risks at both ends of the spectrum. Both escalating recession concerns and retail demand for speculative growth stocks ran against positioning across the industrials and financials sectors. Against this backdrop, fundamentally-focused signals were challenged. Measures of profitability within quality and text-based sentiment insights capturing forward-looking earnings trends detracted. Elsewhere within sentiment, returns from informed investor insights remained volatile amid an ongoing squeeze across popular short positions. Weakness was notable across the IT sector, though returns improved into year-end as investors became more selective across richly-valued names. Previously successful short positions across European consumer stocks moderated as some trade concerns receded.

Average annual total returns (%) as of 12/31/25

4Q25(not annualized) YTD(not annualized) 1 Year 3 Year 5 Years 10 Years
Institutional 5.43 18.36 18.36 15.83 12.47 11.15
Investor A (Without Sales Charge) 5.35 18.08 18.08 15.53 12.19 10.87
Investor A (With Sales Charge) -0.18 11.88 11.88 13.48 10.99 10.27
Morningstar US Fund Large Value Category Avg. 3.15 14.97 14.97 13.88 11.66 10.74
Russell 1000 Value 2 3.81 15.91 15.91 13.90 11.33 10.53

Expenses for Institutional shares: Total 0.71% ; Net, Including Investment Related Expenses (dividend expense, interest expense, acquired fund fees and expenses and certain other fund expenses) 0.54% . For Investor A shares: Total 0.96% ; Net, Including Investment Related Expenses 0.79% . Institutional and Investor A shares have contractual waivers with an end date of 06/30/2027 terminable upon 90 days' notice. For certain share classes, BlackRock may voluntarily agree to waive certain fees and expenses in which the adviser may discontinue at any time without notice. Expenses stated as of the fund's most recent prospectus. Data represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Investment returns and principal values may fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. All returns assume reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Current performance may be lower or higher than that shown. Refer to Advisor Center Resources for US Financial Advisors | BlackRock for most recent month-end performance. Investment returns reflect total fund operating expenses, net of all fees, waivers and/or expense reimbursements. Index performance is shown for illustrative purposes only. It is not possible to invest directly in an unmanaged index. Share classes have different sales charges, fees and other features. Net Expenses Excluding Investment Related Expenses for Institutional shares: 0.54% ; for Investor A shares: 0.79% . Index returns are for illustrative purposes only. Index performance returns do not reflect any management fees or expenses. Indexes are unmanaged and one cannot invest directly in an index. Past performance does not guarantee future results.

Important Risks: The fund is actively managed and its characteristics will vary. Holdings shown should not be deemed as a recommendation to buy or sell securities. Stock values fluctuate in price so the value of your investment can go down depending on market conditions. The fund may use derivatives to hedge its investments or to seek to enhance returns. Derivatives entail risks relating to liquidity, leverage and credit that may reduce returns and increase volatility.

data.title: Pattern Group: Revenue Growth Remains Strong, And The Moat Got Better
data.description:
Summary

I gave a buy rating to Pattern Group (PTRN) in my initiation post, with my key thesis being that PTRN has a solid data moat that should get better as it scales up, and that moat de-risks the growth path ahead. I am even more convinced that PTRN is a buy after looking at recent developments. Revenue growth was strong, newer marketplaces are scaling, and the platform moat looks stronger.

The moat is getting harder to copy

Previously, the main argument for PTRN's moat was that EXP was a strong analytics and optimization tool and that it could help users to optimize the "Traffic x Conversion x Price x Availability" equation. And I think PTRN's moat has gotten a lot better since then. Per management disclosure, the Pattern intelligence layer is now powered by more than 66 trillion data points (vs. 47 trillion six months ago), and more importantly, it was noted that the automation engine executed 5.53 billion marketplace bid changes and 40 million price changes in 2025. These are insanely huge numbers that tell us adoption is real. There are actual entities leveraging PTRN's tool for live pricing and ad decisions, and it is collecting all the data to improve itself.

Analytics and optimization are great, but it is how PTRN helps to execute this operationally that makes it even more interesting. PTRN now has 22 global offices and supports >70 marketplaces, and its products can now reach marketplaces in ~1.5 days. More importantly, days inventory outstanding [DIO] improved to 72 days, down 10 days y/y. My takeaway is that PTRN is able to leverage those 66 trillion data points to further de-risk this inventory-bearing business model, and the numbers speak for themselves. There appears to be no sign of PTRN's pace of scaling slowing either. If PTRN manages to hit 100 trillion data points (should be achieved by this year), can DIO drop further to 60 days? That is possible, and if we extrapolate this, that changes the moat angle a bit. The moat is not just PTRN having better software plus scale anymore. Its moat gradually becomes more like a connected system of data, automation, logistics, and speed. From this angle, competitors would need more than just a dashboard that shows all the analytics and numbers. They would need the same trillions of data points, the marketplace connections, and the physical infrastructure.

New marketplaces are scaling faster

I am also bullish, as PTRN's marketplace rate of scaling up is getting faster and looking more durable. In the call, management said non-Amazon revenue grew 94% y/y in Q4, and international revenue grew 69% y/y. It also said Coupang, TikTok Shop, and Walmart saw triple-digit y/y growth in the quarter. While we don't know the absolute figures, the rate of growth is huge and tells me that these newer channels are contributing real growth. Another data point that convinced me that demand is real is that more than two-thirds of brands now work with PTRN across multiple marketplaces, which also tells you this is becoming part of the operating model rather than a side effort. This matters as it should create a flywheel. As more brands use PTRN across more marketplaces, PTRN gets more experience/data points, which helps in sharpening execution. That should make it easier to bring on board the next brand onto those same marketplaces and easier to move existing brands into even more channels. In other words, each successful rollout is not just driving revenue growth; it is also enhancing PTRN's ability to repeat this.

And I think we can see this "learning experience" showing up in the speed of onboarding. Management said PTRN added 12 marketplaces in FY2025 and also said marketplace number two took a couple of years to build, while newer marketplaces can now be added much faster because the platform has become more modular. One example is Coupang, which went from zero in 2024 to $11 million in 2025. The faster PTRN can get these channels onboard and running, the faster it is able to scale up, which accelerates the flywheel effect and also diversification (which makes the growth more durable).

Valuation

PTRN grew even faster than I thought it could. My initial assumption was FY2025 to see 30% y/y revenue growth, and PTRN beat that by a full 900 bps. That said, I am keeping my forward growth assumptions the same at 30% (rolling 1 year forward to FY2028). Note that management guidance for FY26 revenue growth of ~26% is compared against a high 2025 comp of 39% y/y growth. The momentum is real, and given the enhanced moat and flywheel effect, I stay convinced PTRN can continue to grow rapidly. By my math, compounding PTRN's revenue by ~high-20% from FY26 through FY28 would result in total revenue of ~$5.3 billion in FY28.

For valuation, PTRN's forward revenue multiple has contracted to ~0.5x as I write this update, which I don't think is fair given the growth profile relative to peers. As I mentioned before, peers like HubSpot (HUBS), Salesforce (CRM), EverCommerce (EVCM), and Braze (BRZE) have a much slower revenue growth profile (high single digits to low 20%) vs. PTRN. EVCM is literally trading at ~4x forward revenue with high single-digit revenue growth, for reference. PTRN should easily trade much higher. I am sticking to just 1x for my model, and that is enough to give me an attractive return profile.

Investment Risk

As the world moves towards using AI to help in shopping, it could hurt PTRN's moat. Say that more discovery and checkout move toward AI shopping agents, social commerce, or other channels that are outside traditional third-party marketplaces; PTRN may need to spend more to defend its position (like figuring out how to engage with AI agents, etc.). That would mean more margin pressure that delays the timeline for PTRN to print meaningful profits.

Conclusion

I remain bullish on PTRN because my thesis is playing out. PTRN is still putting up strong growth, the moat looks better, and newer marketplaces are scaling faster. I am expecting PTRN to continue printing this solid growth, and multiples should re-rate upwards in time.

data.title: Dole: Still Expensive, But I'm Raising My Price Target
data.description:
One of the advantages of living a bit everywhere in the world, with extended stays in tropical places, is the access to farm-fresh fruit of the exotic variety. For the past 2 years, I have been eating more mango, pineapple, papaya, passion fruit, jackfruit, and various other fruits that in most areas in the Western World only exist as what I would consider pale imitations (unripe, different in taste, smaller) or preserved.

Now, in order to get the taste when I am in northern climes, I'm left with the canned/preserved variety. Because I like these fruits so much now, I'm left in turn with the best quality of such options.

That was actually one of the reasons I started covering Dole PLC (DOLE) about a year ago, in an article you can find here.

Since that time, Dole has exhibited a fair bit of volatility but has overall performed as I expected it to. It has not generated alpha to its investors (lest you did targeted buying at lower prices more in line with my original PT), and its current RoR to my article sits at about negative 1%, slightly positive if you were to include dividends.

Dole PLC is essentially a play on fruit and, in some cases, vegetables. Since 2021, its formation by the combination of Total and Dole, it has remained one of the largest companies that handles the products of bananas and pineapples, but also more easily obtained produce such as berries and salads. Its market-leading position in both NA and Europe for key tropical fruits that have become staples to consumers makes for an interesting investment opportunity at the right price. Dole is not a company that grocery stores or major grocers can easily go without. It controls the critical infrastructure from the point of farming to the shelf, and the scale, where it actually operates its own shipping services (10+ vessels) and distribution facilities means that it can compete at a level that is nigh impossible for retailers to facilitate themselves, especially the retailers that themselves are geographically far away from areas where growing such produce is even possible.

Even if there are competitors, the scale (again) would make it very hard for these competitors to offer produce at the same price, quality, or shipping because the more intermediaries you include in your process, usually the more the price goes up.

This is what makes Dole attractive, but we also need a good price, and we'll need to look into how the company has been doing as of late.

Dole PLC - Performance in 2025 Leading to 2026E

So, the advantages from an operational perspective of Dole are very clear. Its scale and operations can ensure a year-round supply of what are essentially farm-fresh (with processing, admittedly) staple fruits like pineapples and bananas. Controlling the supply chain means a reduction of costs, which means both higher profit for Dole and higher potential for lower sales prices for end users - a win-win. Furthermore, Dole is typically a trusted brand.

If we look at fundamental issues with its balance sheets or finances, we find a few noteworthy ones. One of them is leverage.

Net debt is still about $1B as of late 2025, as you can see above, though down from about $1.48B in 2021. Why would that be a problem for a business that generates exceeding $9B worth of revenues following the merger?

Because, dear readers, these companies' costs are very high. Conversely, its profits are what I would consider below grocery level. Despite improvements since the merger, when Dole PLC had a margin of only 150 bps (operating level, not net margin, nor EBIT margin (EBIT is non-GAAP), it's still, as of 2025 at below 2.5%. So when you come to ~$215M worth of OI, and when over $65M of that money goes to interest expense, a more than doubling since the period of 2021 (and when another $20M goes to FX), your net income is going to be low. Ergo, the room for interest rate hikes or coverage on interest is much lower than you might expect for a business like this. Much of the debt down payment has been due to divestments, not the company moving net income to pay down - given the relatively low levels of that income.

Net interest for the company was at an all-time low (excepting the negative margins in 2021) and came to 0.6%.

In a sense, the way I choose to interpret this data is that scale has not helped Dole increase its net margins. The company may be, dear readers, a very solid operator with a good supply chain and assets, but it's certainly not all that profitable. Its gross margins are below 10%, and that's below even most grocers.

The issue seems to be that much of the revenue is absorbed by the company's extensive operations. And contrary to what one might expect, the company's pricing power is actually lower than what one might expect, given its commodity-like nature.

Furthermore, Dole has actually struggled with produce (vegetables), a segment that was actually net-negative in terms of profit. This has thankfully been on a turnaround stretch. FX, as you could see from my description above, is also a contributing factor to this. What the company could do fundamentally to combat this is to remove or pay down debt. Given, however, its dividend, which eats up about $34.2M based on about 95M (multiplied by $0.34-$0.36) shares outstanding, and how the other distribution of net income and EBIT looks, the company is very limited in addressing its net debt. At current income levels, even saying that it would take 10 years to pay off is an optimistic estimate, unless the company were to significantly improve its efficiency or profit. I view this as unlikely.

The company refers to its EBITDA for results. However, this excludes cash required for the business by treating recurring expenses as one-time expenses (this is not something that just Dole does; many companies do this). Because of this, I will be focusing strictly on GAAP metrics, such as operating and net income.

I therefore consider, with conviction, the 2025 results reported some time back to be relatively weak. Fruit costs, SCM pressures, and divestiture impacts pressured the company. Dole can do little about rising costs and inflation - these are external factors, but that does not change the fact that they are pressuring the business. Climate risks, such as tropical storms (Sara had a big impact on Dole, apparently). The fact that the progressive produce segment, divested in 2024, has not been replaced has also led to a lower overall revenue and earnings potential.

Meanwhile, while costs and inflation have gone up, Dole has not been able to price products at a higher level. This convergence of costs and margin pressure resulted in a decrease in profitability. Its core segments, pineapples and bananas, were good, but Dole is struggling when it comes to other products, such as cherries, grapes, and others, where the company does not have as big a moat as in the others.

Do not misunderstand me here in believing I am saying that Dole is a bad business to invest in. However, given razor-thin margins and more risks to those margins than I see upside, this demands significant attention to valuation and price prior to investing.

As of 2026, my view is the following.

Dole Valuation - potentially higher in 2026, but not by much

In my last piece, I gave Dole a conservative PT of $12/share. This amounts, for the 2026E period, to about a P/E of 8x, compared to the current P/E of 11.48x. I may have been a bit low there. As of this article, I am raising my PT for the company to $13/share.

My rationale for this increase is as follows.

Dole has been trading at a deep discount compared to its peers. Because recent trends include the company divesting non-core assets, focusing on higher margins, as well as the repayment of debt, this slight increase signifies consideration of that fact.

$13/share comes to a double-digit current P/E of about 10x, without, for that matter, going too high. It puts investors at a P/B of 1x (compared to below 1x), while still deeply discounting its sales due to the margin issues (we're talking around 0.15x to sales, at most 0.2x). It takes into account the company's turnaround potential, but at the same time doesn't put investors in a position where the turnaround being forecasted has to happen. Rather, even in a position where the company continues to trade cheaply due to climate effects or poor results, you're still holding Dole PLC at what I would consider to be a good value.

If I were to describe what a PT of $13/share represents in words, I would say that Dole PLC, at that price, is a "slow-growth grower and trader of fruits" rather than a high-margin multi-field/product player. I believe Dole PLC wants to be viewed as a highly efficient logistical platform, given how it operates. The company may have the potential to get there, but it is not there right now.

In a world where debt is lower or gone, the company could easily reach margins and efficiency, putting it above 5% compared to below 3% today. At that level, I would certainly allow a 13-15x P/E. The reasons that fruit and snack peers, which usually trade at 10x to EBITDA, trade so much higher than Dole PLC (which trades at about 5.5x if we put it at $13/share) and why this is a justified discount, in my view, are weather disruptions, shipping risks, and logistical hurdles, which result in thin profitability aside from its current debt profile.

I believe $13/share more fairly accounts for the company's eventual upside, where I could see a 12-13x P/E, but I believe that buying here would be valuing the company a bit too high.

Risks for the company are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

I view companies through two lenses - historical valuation and peer valuation—and then consider special factors for the company and how it may grow going forward. When it comes to Dole, I like the company, for sure. I just don't like where I am being asked to buy it (in terms of price), given what else is available. The reason for this is related to fundamentals and operational risks, which I consider to be a non-negotiable factor at this point.

Thesis

Dole PLC is a well-known, international brand supplying tropical fruits and produce to geographies around the world. Its main products are fruits/products that cannot grow anywhere but in tropical climates (barring, of course, massive, efficient farming operations with climate control). The company has gone through several changes in the last few years and is now lower-leveraged and with good upside and stability at a conservative investment entry point. However, given some of the risks presented, I believe that the entry point should not be here - even if it's close to that.

I give the company a conservative 9-11x P/E range price, coming to about $13/share to get the upside that I "want" from the company. Because of this, Dole plc is a "hold" to me as of March 2025.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a price target of $13/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of March 2026.

data.title: Karyopharm: Putting The Phase 3 Trial In Context
data.description:
Topline Summary and Update

I've followed Karyopharm Therapeutics (KPTI) for quite a while now with optimism that has not matched reality, placing "Buy" sentiments on the company in 2024 and then later that same year, only to see the valuation of the stock decline by more than 50% as the market has awaited the results of a key, arguably existential phase 3 trial. We now have a first look at the results of that trial, and the market has not been pleased, sending the share price from a high of $8.90 to the near-$5 price point that it sits in at the time of writing. Today, I want to take another look at the company, with the intention of outlining why I think there is reason for optimism here.

Pipeline Updates

Selinexor

For years now, KPTI has been working ardently toward finding new niches for their XPO1 inhibitor selinexor outside of the multiple myeloma space. A readout for endometrial cancer remains a bit on the horizon, with the latest guidance from management suggesting that results should be coming in mid-2026. This relates to the phase 3 XPORT-EC-042 trial evaluating selinexor as a maintenance therapy for TP53 wildtype endometrial cancer; recall that this trial builds on a signal from the SIENDO study, which met its PFS endpoint but was not considered clinically relevant, but a signal of major benefit was identified in the TP53-wildtype subgroup of patients.

So we will wait for the results of XPORT-EC-042 with bated breath. The news we have today relates to a topline readout of the phase 3 SENTRY trial, which compared the addition of selinexor to ruxolitinib versus ruxolitinib alone for patients with treatment-naive myelofibrosis.

The trial met only one of its two primary endpoints. Adding selinexor improved the rate of patients achieving a 35% reduction in spleen size (rates of 50% and 28% for combination and ruxolitinib alone, respectively), but the co-primary endpoint of total symptom score was not improved (9.89 vs 10.86 point improvement).

This actually echoes the findings from MANIFEST-2, a phase 3 study evaluating the addition of the BET inhibitor pelabresib to ruxolitinib in a similar group of patients. Pelabresib plus ruxolitnib had a spleen reduction rate of 65.9% vs 35.2% for ruxolitinib alone, but total symptom score was not different. Notably, pelabresib has not been pushed forward to getting FDA approval at this time, and Novartis is spinning up another phase 3 trial to try pelabresib in patients with higher-risk disease.

For the SENTRY trial, KPTI has seen an interesting signal of improved overall survival with the use of selinexor, with 4.7% vs 10.2% of patients having died in the experimental and control arms, respectively (stratified hazard ratio, 0.43; one-sided nominal P value 0.0222). Median overall survival was 11.56 and 12.58 months, respectively, reflecting how immature these data are.

Reduction of the JAK2 variant allele fraction (a marker of how persistent the disease is) was also observed more frequently with selinexor (32% vs 24%).

Tolerabiliy, as has long been the case with selinexor, was a snag for the study. Nausea rates were increased from 17.2% to 57.3%, and the rate of discontinuing treatment due to AEs increased from 8.6% to 14.5%. Neutropenia and thrombocytopenia rates were also elevated with selinexor therapy.

As part of the presentation, KPTI brought in MPN expert John Mascarenhas to discuss the results, who noted that the overall survival signal was encouraging, and in line with known predictors of overall survival (ie, higher spleen volume response rate, reduction in JAK2 allele burden). He ended with the following:

I believe the combination should be available to all patients, although this may not be the preferred treatment selection for every patient. I look forward to presenting the greater data set from the pivotal trial at upcoming meetings.

That summarizes what is a decided mixed bag of results that KPTI is moving forward with bringing to the FDA for potential approval consideration, per their presentation of the results. A final timeline for their plan toward a supplemental NDA is anticipated within the next 6 months.

Financial Overview

As of the end of 2025, KPTI held $60.5 million in cash and equivalents, along with $3.2 million in investments. They had a senior secured term loan of $21.0 million, as well as various convertible senior notes totaling $111 million. Total revenue for the year was $146.1 million, more or less the same as it was in 2024 and 2023. Operating expenses were $236.8 million, and after interest and extinguishment of debt, the annual net loss was $196 million.

The net cash used for operations was $75.4 million. At this burn rate, KPTI has under one year of funds to continue. This was before the proceeds of an anticipated private placement with RA capital that will bring in $30 million in exchange for shares and warrants.

Strengths and Risks

Mixed - A path to approval, but not one that's certain

I have to be honest: this phase 3 trial is a tough one. Missing one of the primary endpoints is not a good sign for ultimate approval. And that has played out pretty poorly for pelabresib, now finding itself tied to more years of study before it could potentially be added to the mix.

Selinexor appears to be in a similar boat, winning on spleen volume but missing on symptoms. And add the toxicity to boot, and the deck looks stacked against a possible approval.

But this phase 3 trial isn't "tough" because I'm disappointed in the results. It's tough because they have a signal of overall survival improvement that looks encouraging. Even after 96 weeks follow-up for MANIFEST-2, overall survival looked almost completely the same down the line. SENTRY appears to have something to it, and I could potentially see a conditional approval coming on the basis of the positive primary endpoint and this overall survival signal.

However, that's very much not clear to happen at this point, and that does not bode well for the near-term prospects of the investment thesis.

Risk - Dire cash situation even after their private placement

Even with over $100 million in net product revenues, KPTI is facing a cash crisis, and their own guidance during the last corporate update put their runway only "into Q2 2026." Certainly, the $30 million private placement is a shot in the arm, but KPTI has not definitively solved their cash issue with this move, and even under the best circumstances with the FDA, they are looking at a potential approval not coming sooner than late in 2026, and that would be quite an optimistic timeline. How exactly they will continue to fund operations beyond 2026 has not yet been determined, and that makes for a highly risky situation for the investment thesis.

Bottom-Line Summary

KPTI is entering yet another period of uncertainty following the readout of a key phase 3 trial. Will SENTRY yield a new drug approval for selinexor? It's not possible to tell at this point. It's definitely in play, but it's not great to feel this amount of uncertainty about the prospects for this agent.

Couple that with the rather short cash runway that KPTI holds, and it's very easy to see why the market has reacted with substantial pessimism about the value of this stock. And that, honestly, presents the bulk of the opportunity here. KPTI is beaten down, but they now have multiple potential catalysts on deck for 2026 that could present a reversal of fortunes. We will see an initial readout of the endometrial cancer study. We'll get more information about the myelofibrosis study. Positive interactions with the FDA around myelofibrosis, or a positive readout for endometrial cancer (which would, recall, just be a confirmation of the signal from the subgroup analysis of SIENDO) would very likely bring a substantial positive re-evaluation from the market.

And for that reason, I continue to see the near-term upside potential as being worthy of a tentative "Buy" sentiment. That being said, you should not read that as an "unequivocal" recommendation. KPTI is entering into territory that poses existential risks. They have less than a year of cash on hand, and that is not enough time to get to approval. It is likely that they will need to raise funds to continue operations, and there is no guarantee they'll be able to do that.

Therefore, although I am reiterating my "Buy" sentiment at these levels, I would absolutely caution against going too deep into it. And it should be pointed out that the average volume of the stock is relatively low at around 1.2 million shares, which could impede one's ability to enter or exit a position.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Why Credo's Selloff Is A Massive Gift
data.description:
As the AI infrastructure buildout has progressed, the market has focused primarily on the likes of Nvidia (NVDA), AMD (AMD), and Micron (MU) - top semiconductor companies that produce the chips that drive cutting-edge AI models forward. Nvidia's top chips command tens of thousands apiece on the open market, and Micron has recently benefited from a massive shortage in the memory space.

What often gets overlooked are the cables that tie AI data centers together. Fast chips are good, but they're only as good as their ability to talk to other pieces of the cluster.

Enter Credo Technologies (CRDO). As a leading seller of AECs, or "Active Electrical Cables," the firm has been a direct beneficiary of the recent data center buildout. The company's products allow for efficient data center optimization - especially over short distances - and revenue is up 6x over the last two years.

However, following the company's most recent Q3 earnings report (when management announced that sales were up 201% year-over-year), the stock sold off nearly 20%.

Why?

A perceived deceleration in Credo's growth story. On the call, management expressed that forward revenue growth for fiscal year 2027 would "only" be in the ~50% range.

This recent loss compounds with poor year-to-date performance of -30%. All told, I view this dip as a massive gift from the market.

With top and bottom line results set to expand rapidly over the next few years - especially as new products expand the total addressable market - the business is set up to produce significant appreciation for investors.

Trading at a reasonable price versus itself and key peers, I'm anticipating 30%+ annualized total returns through the end of the decade.

Today, I'll break down the company's prospects, touch on the valuation, and make the case that this recent dip in CRDO is a huge opportunity that you should take advantage of.

Financials

In case you haven't heard of Credo before, as I just referenced, the business primarily makes and sells active electrical cables, or AECs, for use in AI data centers. These AECs connect servers and racks, allowing for 1,000x greater link reliability and 50% lower power requirements versus traditional cabling.

In addition to AECs, the company also sells a number of semiconductor-focused integrated circuits, including retimers, DSPs, and chiplets. Tying it all together, CRDO also has a small IP portfolio that generates marginal revenue.

Financially, Credo is growing like a weed. In Q3 of fiscal 2026 (covering November to January), CRDO reported revenue of $407 million, year-over-year growth of 201%, and quarter-over-quarter growth of roughly 52%:

This revenue acceleration ties directly into the pure-play exposure that CRDO has to the data center capex cycle. As big cloud companies like Microsoft (MSFT), Google (GOOG), and Amazon (AMZN) outfit new data centers across the world, they need efficient cables to connect servers together, and CRDO is becoming a favored supplier for this niche.

With structurally strong gross margins coming in at nearly 70%, CRDO is a rare "one-two" punch of a company sporting strong margins and strong growth:

This, in turn, gives management incredible optionality when it comes to deploying fresh capital, and with a debt-free balance sheet, new money coming in is poised to drop directly to the bottom line.

With strong product-market fit and a dialed-in business model, it's no surprise that net income has grown from -$10 million in Q1 of fiscal 2025 to +$157 million in Q3 of fiscal 2026:

Even more importantly, management has been focused on developing new product pillars, including ZF Optics (Zero-Flat), Active LED Cables (ALCs), and OmniConnect gearboxes. Combined, each of these products targets a total addressable market exceeding $10 billion. And, with ongoing momentum and credibility from key hyperscaler customers, I expect these new product launches in fiscal 2027 and 2028 to be received well by the market.

This all adds up to a robust long-term growth outlook.

Currently, I'm modeling 205% growth in fiscal 2026, 52% growth in fiscal 2027, and 27% growth in fiscal 2028. From there, I'm expecting a re-acceleration in sales as Credo's new products begin to cash flow:

Analysts - for their part - map out a relatively similar path.

Valuation

This all maps out to an attractive valuation situation for the stock.

As I alluded to at the start of the article, shares have sold off considerably over the last few months as CRDO sold shares to raise cash, and the deceleration in earnings may have spooked away some institutional money.

However, I view this dip as a massive gift for long-term-oriented folks.

In terms of public comps, I see CRDO's closest peers as Astera Labs (ALAB), Marvell (MRVL), Broadcom (AVGO), and Arista Networks (ANET). At present, these companies trade at an average trailing twelve-month price-to-sales ratio of 18.7x:

Even if you take out Astera Labs, which is by far the most expensive company in the group, this is still an expensive-looking industry, trading at roughly 17.3x sales:

At present, CRDO is trading at the midpoint of the group at 17.8x sales. If we assume a strong finish to the year, then this could shrink further to 14.3x sales, assuming the stock holds at a stable price:

Long-term, if we take CRDO's growth trajectory and assume a 14x sales multiple as a reasonable steady-state expectation, then shares of CRDO could produce roughly 30% annualized returns over the next 48 months:

Zooming out more broadly, I think that a fair value range for this cohort is anywhere from 12.5x to 22x sales, based on historical growth rates, gross margins, and more. For CRDO, this implies a price target of anywhere between $160 and $283 over the next 24 months, representing growth of anywhere between 23% and 61% annualized:

Between the company's strong execution, reasonable entry multiple, and attractive exposure to this technological megatrend, I'm reiterating my "Strong Buy" rating on Credo.

Risks

Of course, there are a number of risks worth mentioning.

First, and most importantly, CRDO has heavy revenue concentration with just a handful of customers within the cloud industry. This exposes the business to changes at just a few companies, in addition to a wider slowdown in cyclical capex.

In Q3, two customers made up roughly 71% of revenue (at 39% and 32%), and a single in-house development push, pause, or architecture decision could cut CRDO out of the value chain, hitting revenue severely. In recent quarters, the company has sought to reduce this risk and recently onboarded another hyperscaler client, bringing the total to five. But broadly, this is the most pressing risk for investors.

Secondly, there are some other risks worth mentioning. On the valuation side, while CRDO is priced reasonably for the semiconductor space, it is relatively expensive versus the broader market, and in the event of a sharp downturn, I could see shares of CRDO getting battered purely as a result of the multiple.

Additionally, high stock-based compensation remains a concern, with SBC representing nearly 20% of the company's overall cost structure:

This promises to dilute shareholders, which could weigh on returns for investors.

Summary

Overall, I view CRDO as an incredibly high-growth opportunity that most investors should be taking advantage of. The recent selloff represents a deceleration after an unbelievable period of growth - not a structural problem with the wider story.

Of course, the company has a number of risks, including significant customer concentration risk, but I view shares - at only 14x blended sales - as an unbelievable deal.

Thus, my "Strong Buy" rating.

Cheers!

data.title: Gold Bearish Trend Resumes Below $4,620 As Stagflation And Oil Strength Weigh
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

Since March 19, gold (XAUUSD:CUR) has staged the expected bearish impulsive down move sequence and plummeted by 15% to print a 4-month low of $4,099 on Monday, March 23, 2026, supported by the “stagflation fear” macro factor.

Thereafter, the previous yellow metal staged a rebound of 12% to hit an intraday high of $4,603 on the backdrop of “TACO” optimism that the US White House Administration is looking to end the month-long US-Iran war, in turn allowing passage to reopen in the Strait of Hormuz, the global oil flow choke point.

Read more over here why "TACO"-induced risk-on sentiment may fail to materialize this time: “Global Markets Swing On U.S.-Iran War Headlines As Risk-On Rally Falters - A Cross Analysis On S&P 500, U.S. Dollar Index, AUD/USD, And WTI Crude”.

Right now, intermarket and technical analyses are pointing to another leg of bearish impulsive down move for gold, likely the end of the 12% corrective rebound, aka dead cat bounce, from Monday’s low.

A firmer WTI crude oil supports further weakness in gold

The macro connection between WTI crude oil and gold is stagflation risk

Higher oil prices via supply-side shock (closure of the Strait of Hormuz leads to a lesser oil supply globally, in turn also creating a second-order effect of lower aggregate demand as input costs of finalized goods and services get more expensive).

Hence, stagflation is a deadly combination of higher prices and lower economic growth prospects in later stages. A challenging environment for central bankers as they cannot easily implement expansionary monetary policies to counter and anticipate the second-order demand destruction in a stagflation environment.

Therefore, central banks are likely to adopt a “wait and see” approach, and some “inflation-fighting” central banks may turn cautiously hawkish and start to implement an interest rate hike cycle.

Gold, being a non-interest income-bearing asset, will incur higher opportunity costs as interest rates rise globally, in turn triggering a negative feedback loop into the price actions of gold.

Since February 17, 2026, the movement of WTI crude oil futures has had an indirect correlation with gold, and its 20-day rolling correlation coefficient stands at -0.5 at this time of writing (see Fig.1).

The recent pullback in the West Texas Oil CFD (a proxy of the WTI crude oil futures) due to “TACO jawbowning” has managed to find support at its rising 20-day moving average.

West Texas Oil CFD’s medium-term uptrend phase remains intact. A clearance above $93.70 key near-term resistance may see a further push up to retest the $102.25 intermediate range resistance in the first step (see Fig. 2).

Gold - End of corrective rebound, start of new bearish leg

Watch the $4,620 key short-term pivotal resistance on gold. A break below the $4,440 key near-term support (downside trigger level) may set off another bearish impulsive down move sequence to retest $4,167/4,099 before exposing the next supports at $4,007 and $3,936/3,886 (also a Fibonacci extension) (see Fig. 3).

On the other hand, a clearance and an hourly close above $4,620 invalidate the bearish scenario for an extension of the corrective rebound towards the $4,737/4,775 key medium-term pivotal resistance zone.

Key elements to support the bearish bias on gold

  • The hourly RSI momentum indicator has staged a bearish breakdown below its key ascending trendline support.
  • The recent 12% rebound seen in gold from its March 23, 2026 low has stalled close to the 50% Fibonacci retracement of the prior impulsive down move from the March 10, 2025 high to the March 23, 2026 low.

Original Post

data.title: EquipmentShare.com: A Much More Compelling Set-Up Here
data.description:
When EquipmentShare.com (EQPT) went public in January, I concluded that rental growth came at a price. Investors liked the rapid growth and philosophy of this rental company, self-described as the future of construction, yet I feared slim operating margins and quite some debt employed, leaving no realistic earnings.

Ever since, a $33 stock has fallen about 40% to its current level at $20 per share; that is less than two months following the offering. This big pullback more than warrants an update, certainly as the company just reported its 2025 results, with the company ending the year on a solid note.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent IPOs, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

About These Results

In the second half of March, EquipmentShare.com reported its 2025 results. Total revenue growth was reported at 16%, with full-year sales reported at $4.38 billion, but that understates the real growth. Equipment rental revenue rose by 34% to $2.72 billion, while equipment sales fell 8% to $1.54 billion. All this was complemented by some smaller equipment parts and supplies revenues. Key rental revenue showed strong growth throughout the year, ending the fourth quarter with 35% growth.

Operating profits of $297 million came in at nearly 7% of sales. Such operating earnings were up 36% on the year before, amidst fundamental margin gains demonstrated upon. That was mostly sufficient to pay a $285 million interest bill, leaving flattish results on the bottom line. That is not really fair, as the company is in massive expansion mode, having opened 95 new sites during 2025, to end the year with 385 operational locations. In fact, the company quantified new market startup costs at $252 million, a huge number in the grand scheme of things.

Growth does not come just at an expense in terms of startup costs, but certainly capital spending and related assets as well. Original equipment costs under management rose to $8.8 billion, but focus on the word "managed" instead of "owned." Net capital spending, net of sales, was seen at just over $600 million last year. Some equipment, about half of the assets, is financed with the "OWN" program, essentially a way to keep some of these assets off-balance sheet.

Net leverage of 3.2 times as of the end of 2025 was seen down to 2.4 times, factoring in proceeds from the IPO, with pro forma net debt seen at $2.34 billion. Note that to arrive at this leverage number, the company adds back a quarter of a billion in new market startup costs to reported EBITDA, that being a fairly aggressive practice.

More Growth

The company sees growth slowing in the number of potential locations in 2026, seen at a midpoint of 425, which suggests 40 net openings, a number more than cut in half from 2025. Total revenues are seen up to $5.05-$5.47 billion, with rental revenues seen between $3.31 and $3.59 billion, all looking quite solid. Despite the slower projected pace of rental growth at 27%, net capital spending is seen increasing to nearly $800 million.

While all this looks good, the adjusted core EBITDA metric is soft. This is seen at a midpoint of $1.87 billion in 2026, just up 12% on the year before, with this number lagging rental growth percentages by more than half, indicating some margin pressure. All this indicates that the business will see rapid growth, but realistic earnings likely will not be seen in 2026 as well. Note that this metric for EBITDA is much larger, this adding the EBITDA contribution from the OWN program asset as well, which is roughly half of total assets, these essentially being kept off-balance sheet.

By now, 251 million shares have fallen some $13 since I covered shares in January, shedding well over $3 billion in value, that being a big pullback here. Currently, the market value stands at $5.0 billion at $20 per share, and including net debt of $2.4 billion, the enterprise valuation stands at $7.4 billion.

This $7.4 billion enterprise valuation comes in at 2.7 times rental revenues and just over 2 times rental revenues based on the guidance. This starts to look a lot more compelling here, as undisputed industry leader United Rentals (URI) is valued at just over 4 times rental revenues, but posts operating income around 25% (that includes both rental revenue and sales of rental equipment).

What Now?

The reality is that I like the approach of the business, focusing heavily on providing innovation to the building site. Suffering from idled machines and outdated (paper) processes, the industry has plenty of opportunity to improve utilization rates with dynamic prices, smart scheduling, and preventive maintenance performed with real-time data, purpose-built software, and related processes.

Given the discussion above, there are some things to like. For one, is continued growth in the final quarter, but moreover, a setback in expectations following a 40% pullback in the share price. While the company is essentially breaking even, I like the continuation of growth in 2026, yet the divergence (with much lower adjusted EBITDA growth) is worrying.

This makes me take a wait-and-see approach here, yet I feel more comfortable at current lower levels. Break-even levels are achieved, while earnings are depressed by growth initiatives and the fact that relative sales multiples now look compelling (recognizing the huge margin gap, of course), as I like the organic growth reported here. On the negative side, real earnings are not yet seen, as the OWN Program complicates things a bit.

Weighing it all together, I am turning more upbeat here, mostly given the lower expectations. However, the discrepancy between core adjusted EBITDA growth and the lagging sale price is significant. I am not convinced to buy the dip just yet, looking for more clues about these margin trends as 2026 progresses.

data.title: Money Supply Grows At Fastest Pace Since 2021
data.description:
Money supply is a very important indicator. It helps show how tight or loose current monetary conditions are regardless of what the Fed is doing with interest rates. Even if the Fed is tight, if money supply is increasing, it has an inflationary effect.

One key metric shown below is the “Wenzel” 13-week annualized money supply figure. It was made popular by the late Robert Wenzel who tracked the metric weekly as an indicator for where the economy might be headed. In 2020, the Fed started reporting the data monthly instead of weekly. It should also be noted that money supply data can be heavily revised in future months.

Recent Trends

Seasonally adjusted money supply has been growing on a consistent monthly basis since November 2023 (28 straight months). The latest month (February) showed a massive increase of $198B. This is more than double the recent trend, which is quite alarming. What has caused this sudden increase in money supply? It likely has many drivers, but the fact the Fed has restarted QE could be a major reason.

The increase in February was 11.1% annualized, which is well above the 4.9% over the past year.

February average is typically around 6%, which puts the latest month still well above average.

Non-seasonally adjusted shows negative growth for the month of January and positive growth for February and March (note: this data is ahead of the seasonally-adjusted data above).

The weekly data shows what happened: the recent data has been moving with more consistency rather than exhibiting a more choppy behavior as it did for most of the past.

The “Wenzel” 13-week Money Supply

The late Robert Wenzel of Economic Policy Journal used a modified calculation to track money supply. He used a trailing 13-week average growth rate annualized as defined in his book The Fed Flunks. He specifically used the weekly data that was not seasonally adjusted. His analogy was that in order to know what to wear outside, he wants to know the current weather, not temperatures that have been averaged throughout the year.

The objective of the 13-week average is to smooth some of the choppy data without bringing in too much history that could blind someone from seeing what’s in front of them. The 13-week average growth rate can be seen in the table below.

Growth has been relatively flat for several weeks now, around the 6% range.

The plot below shows how the latest year compares with previous years. Typically, this is the time of year when money supply growth starts to dip. However, it seems that this year is remaining much more flat.

Inflation and Money Supply

The chart below shows the history of inflation, money supply, and Fed funds. As shown, in 1970 inflation worked with a ~2-year lag compared to money supply. Money supply slowed dramatically in 2023 and 2024 but has been moving back up. Inflation has also been stickier than the Fed would like, but unfortunately, they cannot do much given the large debt load of the US Government and corporations. Despite inflation staying elevated, even moving up in the recent period, the Fed has no choice but to continue lowering rates.

The inflation rate is now only ~1% below the fed funds rate. Most importantly, the inflation rate is no longer falling like it has been for the last few years. The rate has remained above 2% for 4 years now, proving much stickier than anyone originally forecast. Furthermore, the market is still pricing in a rate cut before a rate hike, all but ensuring the CPI stays elevated.

Historical Perspective

The charts below are designed to put the current trends into historical perspective. The orange bars represent annualized percentage change rather than raw dollar amount. You can see that the money supply is rising at the fastest clip since 2021. This is not a good sign if inflation needs to be coming down.

Below shows the 13-week annualized average over history. This chart overlays the log return of the S&P. Mr. Wenzel proposed that large drops in money supply could be a sign of stock market pullbacks. His theory, derived from Murray Rothbard, states that when the market experiences a shrinking growth rate of money supply (or even negative), it can create liquidity issues in the stock market, leading to a sell-off.

While not a perfect predictive tool, many of the dips in money supply precede market dips. Specifically, the major dips in 2002 and 2008 from +10% down to 0%. 2022 was highly correlated with a fall in money supply, and the rebound has corresponded with the big stock market move we saw in 2023 and into 2024. The slowing growth in 2025 did correlate with the market drop in March/April, but that was clearly more headline-driven.

The market has been very choppy since October. Considering the money supply has continued to grow over that time, the compressed range in the stock markets is likely to result in a move higher once we see a breakout of the current range. Obviously, the war has thrown a monkey wrench into things, but if there is any resolution to the war, it could create a market breakout.

Please note the chart only shows market data through Mar 2nd to align with available M2 data.

One other consideration is the reverse repo market at the Fed. This is a tool that allows financial institutions to swap cash for instruments on the Fed balance sheet.

Reverse repos peaked at $2.55T on Dec 30, 2022. Money gushed out from March 2023 to May 2024. The balance now sits close to zero.

Wrapping Up

The CPI remains above 2%. With a war in the Middle East driving up energy prices and a continued money supply expansion, there is a near certainty that the CPI will be trending up in the months ahead and not down. Then the Fed is in a real pickle. Do they reverse course and start raising rates, leave them unchanged, or continue to cut? What if the labor market weakens? The next Fed chair will be a big driver of policy. Will they prioritize price stability or the labor market? Given the massive debt and over-levered economy, it is almost a certainty that rates will not be raised and the easy money spigots will continue flowing. This could be the final end to the dollar.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
